title: Dog Seizure in Young Puppies: Causes, Symptoms, and Emergency Care
meta_desc: Seeing a puppy seizure is frightening. Learn why this is a high-priority emergency and how to help your puppy during and after a seizure.
content_html:
TL;DR: Any seizure in a young puppy is a high-level medical emergency that requires immediate veterinary care to prevent life-threatening complications like low blood sugar. If possible, capture a video of the event to help your veterinarian diagnose the cause more quickly.

What does a seizure look like in a young puppy?

A seizure is a sudden, involuntary electrical disturbance in the puppy's brain. In young dogs, this may look like 'paddling' with their legs, snapping at invisible flies, stiffening of the body, or loss of bowel and bladder control. It is a sign that something is interrupting normal brain function, which can be particularly delicate in developing puppies.

Is a puppy seizure a medical emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High.
  • Yes, any seizure in a young puppy is considered a medical emergency.
  • Puppies have very small energy reserves; a seizure can quickly lead to life-threatening hypoglycemia (low blood sugar) or hyperthermia (overheating).
  • Contact your veterinarian or the nearest emergency animal hospital immediately, even if the puppy seems to have recovered.

How does a video of the seizure help my veterinarian?

  • If you can safely do so, capturing a short video of the seizure is one of the most helpful things you can provide to the veterinary team.
  • Many other conditions, such as fainting or tremors caused by toxins, can look like seizures.
  • A video allows the vet to see the exact nature of the movements, which helps them determine the cause and the most effective treatment plan much faster.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In young puppies, seizures can be caused by inherited conditions like polymicrogyria in Standard Poodles, which manifests with visual disturbances, ataxia, behavioral problems, and hydrocephalus. Hepatic encephalopathy, often due to congenital portosystemic shunts, is another cause, particularly in Miniature Schnauzers, Yorkshire Terriers, Cairn Terriers, Australian Cattle Dogs, Old English Sheepdogs, and Maltese Terriers, with clinical signs of cerebral dysfunction such as staring into space or inappropriate vocalizing typically appearing before 6 months of age. Status epilepticus necessitates immediate treatment to prevent life-threatening complications like hyperthermia, acidosis, hypoperfusion, and hypoxia, with diazepam being the preferred drug for rapid seizure control.

Chapter: Neurology, Internal Medicine

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1224)

faqs.question: What are the most common causes of seizures in puppies?
faqs.answer: Common causes in young puppies include low blood sugar (hypoglycemia), ingestion of household toxins, liver shunts, or infectious diseases such as distemper.

faqs.question: What should I do while my puppy is having a seizure?
faqs.answer: Keep them safe by moving them away from stairs or sharp corners. Do not put anything in their mouth, as puppies do not swallow their tongues and you may get bitten. Focus on timing the seizure and keeping the environment quiet.

faqs.question: How will a vet treat my puppy's seizure?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on the cause. The vet may administer glucose if blood sugar is low, use anticonvulsant medications to stop the seizing, and perform blood work to check for underlying health issues.
